# Bayes' theorem with discrete models

Bayesian inference is just bookkeeping with probability. We start with what we
believe *before* seeing data (the **prior**), multiply by how well each
hypothesis explains the data (the **likelihood**), and renormalise to get what
we believe *after* (the **posterior**).

## A marine example

An acoustic tag pings and is picked up by receivers, but the detection pattern
is ambiguous: the fish could have come from the **North**, **Mid**, or **South**
reef. From past tracking we know how likely that detection pattern is from each
reef — these are the **likelihoods**.

## The theorem, term by term

$$
\underbrace{P(\text{reef} \mid \text{data})}_{\text{posterior}}
=
\frac{
  \overbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \text{reef})}^{\text{likelihood}}\;
  \overbrace{P(\text{reef})}^{\text{prior}}
}{
  \underbrace{\sum_{r} P(\text{data} \mid \text{reef}_r)\,P(\text{reef}_r)}_{\text{denominator}}
}
$$

- The **numerator** for each reef is just `prior × likelihood`.
- The **denominator** is *the sum of the numerators across all reefs*. It is the
  total probability of the data, and it is what makes the posterior add to 1.

That second point is the key idea: **the denominator is not a separate mystery
quantity — it is built from the same numerators you already computed.**

## Survey weight as a prior

In fisheries and marine ecology, survey data often come with a **survey weight**
— a number that reflects how much effort was spent sampling each area, or how
representative each observation is of the population. This weight is a natural
candidate for the prior.

Suppose the three reefs were surveyed unevenly: the North reef was sampled on
60 % of survey trips, the Mid reef on 30 %, and the South reef on 10 %. Before
you even look at the acoustic data, you already know a fish is *more likely* to
come from the North reef simply because it was sampled more heavily. The survey
weight encodes that prior belief.

Setting $P(\text{reef}) \propto \text{survey weight}$ is then a principled
choice: you are saying "my prior is proportional to how often I looked there."
When the acoustic evidence is strong, the data will still dominate and the
posterior will differ from the prior. When the evidence is weak or ambiguous,
the posterior will stay close to the survey weight — exactly the behaviour you
want.

This connection appears throughout Bayesian ecology:

| Prior | What it encodes |
|-------|----------------|
| Uniform ($P = 1/n$ for each option) | Complete ignorance — all hypotheses equally plausible |
| Survey weight | Sampling effort — some areas observed more than others |
| Historical detection rate | Past experience about where animals are found |

The choice of prior is *an explicit modelling decision*. Stating
it as a survey weight makes that decision transparent and auditable.

## Your task

In the challenge below, fill in the numerator for each reef, then the
denominator (the sum), then each posterior. Then switch the prior and watch the
posterior change — same data, different starting beliefs.
